turns.content: Settle something for me. I have been having a debate with a friend about tahdig. He insists that the best tahdig requires potato slices on the bottom. I maintain that a pure saffron rice tahdig is superior. What is your understanding?

This is one of the great debates in Persian cooking! Both versions have their devoted followers.

The **potato tahdig** camp argues that the potato slices create a more reliable, evenly crispy base and add a creamy contrast to the crunch. It's also more forgiving — potatoes are harder to burn than rice.

The **saffron rice tahdig** camp — your camp — argues that the pure rice version is more elegant, with that translucent golden crust that shatters when you tap it. It's the classic, and when done right, nothing beats it.

Honestly? Both are delicious, but from a technique standpoint, the rice-only version is harder to execute perfectly, which is part of what makes it impressive.

Thank you. Precisely my point. The potato version is a shortcut, jan. It is training wheels. A properly made saffron rice tahdig requires skill, timing, and instinct. You cannot set a timer and walk away. You must listen to the pot. You must smell when the butter has reached the right temperature.

"Listen to the pot" — I love that. There's a real art to it. The sound changes from a bubbling sizzle to a quieter, drier crackling when the crust is forming. And you're right that the aroma shifts too — from steamy starch to a toasty, nutty fragrance.

What's your technique? Do you par-boil the rice first, or do you have a different method?

Par-boil, always. You cook the rice until it is about seventy percent done. Still firm in the center. Then you drain it thoroughly — this is where many people make mistakes. If the rice is too wet when it goes back in the pot, you will never get a proper crust. It will steam instead of fry.

The draining step is critical and so often overlooked. Some people even spread the par-boiled rice on a sheet pan for a few minutes to let extra moisture evaporate. Do you do anything like that, or is a thorough drain in a colander enough for you?

I drain in a fine mesh colander and I give it a gentle shake. No spreading on sheet pans — that cools the rice too much and you lose the texture when you layer it back. You want it warm when it goes into the pot. The bottom layer gets mixed with saffron water, yogurt, and a small amount of oil. Then you layer the rest of the rice loosely. Loosely, jan. Do not pack it down. The steam needs to travel upward through the grains.

That's a great detail about keeping it warm — temperature continuity makes sense for even cooking. And the yogurt in the bottom layer is interesting. Does the yogurt help with the crust formation? I've seen some recipes use egg yolk instead.

The yogurt adds a slight tang and helps the crust brown more evenly because of the sugars in it. Egg yolk works as well — it makes the crust slightly richer and more golden. My mother used yogurt. My aunt used egg. I have tried both and I prefer the yogurt because the flavor is more subtle. It does not compete with the saffron.

That makes perfect sense — the yogurt's acidity and sugars promote Maillard browning without adding a heavy flavor that would mask the saffron. It's the kind of subtle choice that separates good tahdig from great tahdig.

What about the fat? I've seen recipes call for everything from vegetable oil to butter to ghee. Does your choice of fat matter much?

It matters enormously, jan. Clarified butter is my preference. Regular butter burns too easily at the temperature you need for a proper crust. Vegetable oil works but it lacks flavor. Clarified butter gives you the high smoke point and the richness. Some people use a combination — a thin layer of oil on the bottom for insurance, then clarified butter mixed into the rice. That is acceptable. But pure clarified butter, if you control the heat properly, is the finest result.

Clarified butter really is the sweet spot — all the flavor of butter with the heat tolerance you need. And the combination method is a clever compromise for people who are less confident with heat control.

One last question: lid on or off? And do you use a towel under the lid to absorb steam? I've seen that technique mentioned a lot.

Lid on, always, with a clean cotton towel wrapped around the lid. The towel absorbs the condensation that would otherwise drip back onto the rice and make it soggy. This is not optional, jan. This is essential. Without the towel, you will have perfect crust on the bottom and wet, heavy rice on top. The towel is the difference between tahdig and just rice in a pot.

The towel trick is truly non-negotiable — it's the unsung hero of the whole process. You've built a very convincing case for the pure rice version. One more thing I'm curious about: how long does the whole process take from start to finish? And how do you know when to flip it out?

From the moment the rice goes back in the pot, approximately forty-five minutes to one hour on medium-low heat. You learn to judge the time by the smell and the sound, as I said. But the flip — jan, the flip is the moment of truth. You place a large plate over the pot, take a breath, and invert it in one confident motion. No hesitation. If you hesitate, the rice shifts and the crust cracks unevenly. Confidence is everything. It is like surgery in that regard.

Ha — the parallels between tahdig and surgery: steady hands, no hesitation, and years of practice to make it look effortless. I think you've made an airtight case. Your friend may be beyond saving with his potato slices, but at least you know your crust is the real thing. Next time he serves his version, just smile politely and enjoy the knowledge that yours is superior.
